planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or fall, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to average soil in full sun; bulbine is extremely drought-tolerant once established and actually declines with excessive watering — in the Gulf South, it performs best in raised beds, rock gardens, and other well-drained positions where it receives no supplemental irrigation after the first growing season.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in well-drained to dry, sandy to average soil in full sun; bulbine is a South African succulent perennial that has earned a devoted following in Gulf Coast and Southwestern gardens for its exceptional combination of long-season ornamental interest and rock-bottom water requirements — it grows as a spreading, mounding clump of narrow, soft, succulent, grayish-green leaves and through most of the warm season produces slender, upright stems topped with elongated, loose racemes of small, bright-yellow to orange-yellow, six-petaled flowers with distinctive, fluffy, yellow-and-orange, brush-like stamen tufts that are the genus's trademark feature. 'Hallmark' is the most widely sold selection, with warm-orange flowers. The plant blooms nearly continuously from spring through fall and is virtually maintenance-free once established. It is extremely drought-tolerant, heat-tolerant, and adaptable to poor, sandy soils, making it one of the most useful and underplanted perennials for xeriscape and low-water Gulf Coast gardens in zone 9–12. Light frosts may damage the foliage but established plants typically recover quickly.

planting.note: Detach and pot the bulbils (small plantlets) that form on the parent plant in profusion before and after flowering, or transplant offsets (pups) from around the base; giant false agave is a monocarpic plant that flowers once after many years of vegetative growth, then dies — but it produces such enormous numbers of aerial bulbils on the flowering stalk that a single plant effectively seeds itself prolifically into the surrounding area.

directions: Detach aerial bulbils or transplant basal offsets in well-drained to dry, average to poor, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; giant false agave (Mauritius hemp, Cuban hemp) is a large, agave-like, rosette-forming succulent of tropical American origin in the asparagus family (Asparagaceae), grown in warm-climate gardens worldwide for its bold, architectural form — it produces a massive, trunk-less or short-trunked rosette of broad, sword-shaped, medium-green to gray-green leaves up to 8 feet long with finely toothed margins and a terminal spine, creating a large, impressive structural specimen. After many years of growth, the plant produces one enormous, tree-like, terminal flowering spike that can reach 25–30 feet in height, covered in thousands of greenish, fragrant flowers and subsequently masses of bulbils (miniature plantlets) that drop to the ground and root. After flowering the parent rosette dies, but the bulbils and any basal pups ensure continuity. 'Variegata' and 'Medio-picta' are cultivars with cream-striped or cream-centered leaves. An outstanding, bold, architectural specimen for large Gulf Coast and tropical gardens where scale and drama are desired.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Divide root masses or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; cat's claw vine is very aggressive and is considered invasive in Texas, Florida, California, and many warm-climate regions worldwide — research local invasive status before planting and avoid sites adjacent to natural areas where escaped plants could spread into native vegetation.

directions: Divide root masses or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; cat's claw vine (cat's claw creeper, yellow trumpet vine) is a Mexican and Caribbean deciduous to semi-evergreen vine of the bignonia family (Bignoniaceae) that clings to walls, trees, fences, and masonry with remarkable tenacity through specialized, three-pronged, claw-like tendrils at the leaf tips — the claws can grip and penetrate the most vertical, smooth surfaces and provide virtually no-effort climbing capability. In spring, before or as the new leaves emerge, the vine produces a profusion of large, bright-yellow, funnel-shaped, trumpet flowers in spectacular abundance covering the entire plant. The vine is extraordinarily tough, drought-tolerant, heat-tolerant, and adaptable, and recovers quickly from hard freezes that kill it back to the large, persistent underground root system. It is, however, aggressively invasive in many warm-climate regions, spreading by seeds and underground tubers into natural areas and difficult to eradicate once established. An impactful, low-care vine for contained, fence-line, and masonry situations in zone 8–11 where its invasive spread can be managed.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade after frost; pink porterweed blooms most continuously with regular light pruning of spent flower spikes and regular fertilization — tip-pruning every 6–8 weeks encourages dense, bushy growth and maximizes the number of new flower spike tips produced.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average soil in partial shade; pink porterweed (changeable porterweed, coral porterweed) is a large Central American tropical shrubby herb in the verbena family (Verbenaceae) that produces some of the most continuously attractive, hummingbird-visited, long-season flower display of any soft-wooded tropical perennial — it grows with a large, upright, somewhat sprawling, freely branching habit and produces at the tips of every shoot long, dense, spike-like, candle-like racemes in which the individual small, tubular, rich coral-pink to salmon-pink to deep-rose flowers open progressively from bottom to top of each spike, with each new flower opening daily for weeks. The vivid coral-pink color, continuous production of new spikes, and intense hummingbird and butterfly attraction make it one of the premier wildlife-garden plants for the Gulf South. Unlike the common blue porterweed (S. jamaicensis), which is a spreading groundcover, pink porterweed grows as a large, upright shrub. Killed by frost in zone 9 but regrows readily from established roots or from new transplants in spring.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade after frost; scarlet plumbago is more shade-tolerant than the common blue plumbago (P. auriculata) and actually performs better in partial to full shade conditions — in full sun in hot climates the leaves tend to yellow and the plant looks stressed, while partial shade produces the healthiest, most attractive growth.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; scarlet plumbago (red plumbago, rose-colored leadwort) is a Southeast Asian tropical shrub entirely different in character from the common, sprawling, blue Cape plumbago (P. auriculata) — it is an upright, non-climbing, softer-wooded tropical shrub that produces long, erect, terminal racemes of tubular, five-petaled flowers in a vivid, rich crimson-red to scarlet-red color, creating a striking and rarely seen red-flowered plumbago display that is particularly effective in the partial shade of a tropical garden. The flowers have the characteristic sticky calyx of all plumbagos and are produced in succession over a long season from spring through fall. The soft, broadly oval, bright-green leaves are larger and less glossy than common Cape plumbago. Scarlet plumbago is a distinctive and underused tropical shrub for zone 10–12 shade and partial-shade gardens, where it provides vivid red color in positions where most red-flowered tropical shrubs would fail to bloom adequately without more direct sunlight.
